book_claims.claim: Slow fermentation reduces phytate levels, increasing iron and zinc absorption
book_claims.mechanism: Lactic acid bacteria in sourdough starter produce phytase enzymes that degrade phytic acid during long fermentation, releasing bound minerals

supplementary_research.finding: Randomized crossover trial (Scazzina et al., 2019) found sourdough bread produced significantly lower glycemic and insulinemic responses compared to bread made with baker's yeast, confirming metabolic advantages of slow fermentation.

Systematic review (Dimidi et al., 2019) found sourdough fermentation reduced phytic acid by 24-70%, significantly improving mineral bioavailability of iron, zinc, and magnesium compared to conventional yeast bread.

Review (Gobbetti et al., 2019) found that sourdough lactobacilli partially degrade gluten during fermentation and produce exopolysaccharides that improve gut microbiome composition, suggesting benefits beyond mineral bioavailability.

culinary_pairings.source: Classic bread dipping; fat slows carbohydrate absorption
Bread + bean soups for complete protein
Fermented + fermented pairing; calcium and protein complement
Garlic rubbed on toasted bread (fettunta); allicin + mineral-rich bread

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Sourdough fermentation degrades 24-70% of phytic acid, making iron 2-3x more absorbable than in conventional bread.
Phytate reduction during fermentation significantly improves zinc bioavailability compared to yeast-leavened bread.
Sourdough fermentation increases folate content by 2-3x compared to conventional bread through microbial synthesis.

synergies.description: Sourdough whole grain bread far superior to white bread
